planting.note: Direct sow on the surface of dry, lean, sandy or gravelly soil after last frost; press seed into soil but do not cover deeply, as light aids germination.

directions: A bold, spiny-stemmed native annual or biennial poppy with deeply lobed, blue-gray, spine-tipped leaves and enormous, tissue-paper-white flowers 3-4 inches across with golden-yellow stamens; native to dry, open prairies, roadsides, and disturbed ground of the Great Plains and Rocky Mountain foothills. All parts of the plant are toxic if ingested and the milky yellow sap causes skin irritation — handle with thick gloves. Direct sow in spring in dry, lean, sandy or gravelly, well-drained soil in full sun; self-sows prolifically and naturalizes in open, disturbed, dry ground. An excellent, drought-tolerant native annual for large-scale prairie plantings.
sources: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center: Plains Prickly Poppy

planting.note: Direct sow at or just after last frost in dry, sandy, well-drained soil; seed requires warmth to germinate reliably.

directions: A freely self-seeding annual prickly poppy native to tropical Mexico and the Caribbean, now widely naturalized in disturbed, dry, open ground throughout the southern US and tropical regions worldwide; produces deeply lobed, spine-tipped, silvery-veined, blue-green leaves and golden-yellow, 4-6-petaled flowers 1.5-2 inches across from spring through fall. The yellow sap and seeds are toxic — do not ingest. Direct sow in spring in dry, sandy, well-drained, lean soil; self-sows prolifically and can become weedy in favorable conditions. Grown in folk medicine traditions across tropical America for its analgesic properties.

planting.note: Direct sow on the surface of bare, dry, lean, sandy or gravelly soil around last frost; does not transplant well due to a sensitive taproot.

directions: A southeastern US native prickly poppy of dry, sandy or rocky, disturbed open ground from Virginia to Florida and Texas; produces spiny, deeply lobed, silvery-veined, blue-green leaves and pure white, 6-petaled, crinkled-tissue-paper flowers 2-3 inches across with prominent yellow stamens from spring through summer. The yellow sap is toxic if ingested. Direct sow in spring on bare, dry, lean, sandy or gravelly, well-drained soil in full sun; does not transplant well and self-sows readily in open ground. An excellent low-care annual for dry, naturalistic Gulf Coast gardens and native roadsides.
sources: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center: White Prickly Poppy

planting.note: Direct sow in fall to allow cold stratification over winter; seeds require a cold, moist period to break dormancy reliably in spring.

directions: A native perennial prickly poppy of dry, rocky slopes, canyon walls, and disturbed open ground in the Rocky Mountains and Great Plains; more persistently perennial than annual Argemone species; produces white, tissue-paper-textured flowers 2-3 inches across on densely spiny, branching stems with blue-gray, deeply lobed, spine-tipped leaves. The yellowish sap is toxic. Direct sow in fall in rocky, very well-drained, lean, dry soil in full sun; established plants are extremely drought-tolerant and long-lived. Excellent for rocky slopes and xeriscape gardens in zones 4-9.
sources: Flora of North America: Argemone hispida

planting.note: Direct sow in fall in California; seed requires cool, moist conditions to germinate and blooms in spring before summer heat arrives.

directions: A delicate California native annual related to the California poppy, producing slender, upright stems with finely cut leaves and vivid brick-red to salmon-orange, 4-petaled flowers with a deep maroon basal blotch in spring; native to grassy chaparral burns, open hillsides, and disturbed ground of California's Coast Ranges and foothills. Often appears in abundance after wildfire, earning it the name 'fire follower.' Direct sow in fall in dry, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy or loamy soil in full sun; does not transplant well. An outstanding, rarely cultivated annual for California native gardens and fire-follower wildflower mixes.
